Client 2: Yeah, and just… a clear plan. I don’t want it to feel complicated.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start high level with cash flow, then we’ll inventory accounts and any insurance. Jennifer, what do you have down for total household gross income for the year?
Client 1: Household gross income is 91800 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing sources, can you walk me through what makes that up?
Client 1: Sure. I have business income of 53850 a year, plus a bonus that totals 22050 annually, and Social Security is 15900 annually.

Client 2: And I’ve got income too—mine is 31000 a year, even though I’m not working right now.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Now on the spending side—about what are your total monthly expenses, all-in?
Client 1: Total monthly expenses are 2700.
Advisor: 2,700 a month—okay. Does that include the alimony payments you mentioned when you booked the meeting, or is that separate?
Client 1: It’s included in the 2700. I keep it all in one bucket.

Client 2: Yeah, and just… a clear plan. I don’t want it to feel complicated.

Client 1: Yes, monthly debt payments total 0.
Advisor: Nice. Let’s shift to what you’ve built up. Jennifer, roughly what do you consider your investable assets—excluding the house?
Client 1: Investable assets total 688350.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that down so I understand where it sits. What’s the big taxable or brokerage-type bucket?
Client 1: I have 441400 in a taxable account at a bank.

Client 2: I do—73250 in private markets, also on the advisor platform.
Advisor: Great, I’ll note that. Now, separate from investable assets, tell me about the home—do you consider it your primary residence, and what’s the value you’re using?
Client 1: Yes, it’s our primary residence, and the value is 411500.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to close the loop on liabilities—no mortgage against that house, correct?
Client 1: Correct, no mortgage.

Client 2: Yeah, and just… a clear plan. I don’t want it to feel complicated.

Advisor: Got it. And country—just confirming you’re based in the US?
Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one is more about how we work together. Are you engaging me through an RIA—registered investment advisor relationship—or something else?
Client 1: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Thanks—just wanted to make sure we’re aligned there. Now, risk comfort: when markets get choppy, do you two generally want to protect principal first, even if returns are a bit lower?

Client 1: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Thanks—just wanted to make sure we’re aligned there. Now, risk comfort: when markets get choppy, do you two generally want to protect principal first, even if returns are a bit lower?
Client 2: Yeah, that’s us—conservative.
Advisor: Okay. And Jennifer, same answer for you, or do you see it differently?
Client 1: Same—conservative. I’m fine with measured growth, but I don’t want big drawdowns.

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi Robert—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. A win is getting everything on the table—income, expenses, assets—and making sure there aren’t any surprises later.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… a clear plan. I don’t want it to feel complicated.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start high level with cash flow, then we’ll inventory accounts and any insurance. Jennifer, what do you have down for total household gross income for the year?

Client 2: Yes—number of adults is 2.
Advisor: Perfect. And relationship status—how should I describe it for planning purposes? Married, remarried, partnered…
Client 1: We’re secondly wedded.
Advisor: Thanks. That helps with how we think about benefits and, honestly, just the way we frame goals together.
Advisor: Switching gears slightly—taxes. Do you know what bracket you typically land in these days, Jennifer? Even a ballpark is fine.

Client 2: I always mix this up—like, is that the same as what comes out of the paycheck?
Advisor: Close, but not exactly. Withholding is what comes out during the year; your tax bracket is the rate band you’re in when you file. Do you know the bracket you usually fall into?
Client 1: Yes—tax bracket band is 24%.
Advisor: Great, thank you. One sec—just noting that.
Advisor: And since we’re here, I’m going to do a quick sanity check that we’ve touched each bucket: income, assets, liabilities, and protection. Income was your Social Security, bonus, and business income, Jennifer; assets included the bank taxable cash, the retirement account, the other advisor-platform bank-style account, Robert’s private markets, and the home; liabilities were zero across the board; and protection is that long-term care policy. Still all consistent?

Client 1: It’s tied to the business—same thing we covered earlier.
Advisor: Understood. What gross annual income should I use for you, Jennifer?
Client 1: My gross annual income is 60800.
Advisor: Thanks—before we move on, I just want to make sure I’ve got the right names matched to my notes so nothing gets crossed later. Jennifer, you’re Client 1, correct?
Client 1: Yes—client label is 1.

Advisor: Hi Jennifer, hi Robert—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?

Client 1: No, that’s right—household role is primary.
Advisor: Great. And employment-wise, Jennifer—what’s the best description of your current status?
Client 1: I’m employed.
Advisor: And is that employment tied to the business income we discussed, or do you also have a separate W-2 job?
Client 1: It’s tied to the business—same thing we covered earlier.

Advisor: Robert, do you have anything in your name that we should include in the investable pile?
Client 2: I do—73250 in private markets, also on the advisor platform.
Advisor: Great, I’ll note that. Now, separate from investable assets, tell me about the home—do you consider it your primary residence, and what’s the value you’re using?
Client 1: Yes, it’s our primary residence, and the value is 411500.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to close the loop on liabilities—no mortgage against that house, correct?

Client 2: Not employed right now. I wouldn’t call it retired-retired, but yeah—inactive.

Client 2: Business owner.
Advisor: Thanks. Now income—what gross annual income should I use for you, Robert? Even if it’s a mix of sources, I just need the total gross for planning.
Client 2: It’s about thirty-ish. The exact gross annual income is 31000.

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not mixing sources, can you walk me through what makes that up?

Advisor: Okay, that helps. Now on the spending side—about what are your total monthly expenses, all-in?

Advisor: Perfect. And relationship status—how should I describe it for planning purposes? Married, remarried, partnered…

Advisor: Thanks. That helps with how we think about benefits and, honestly, just the way we frame goals together.

Client 2: I’m the spouse/partner.
Advisor: Got it. And work status right now—are you working, retired, between roles, something else?
Client 2: I’m inactive at the moment—so not working.

Client 2: Not employed right now. I wouldn’t call it retired-retired, but yeah—inactive.
